steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage sets up a contrast: some ethicists say morality is context-independent (actions are judged on their own, regardless of circumstances). Purcell finds the opposite in Aztec philosophy: actions are judged by how well they fit the individual's social role and contribution to community. The blank must complete a logical inference from Purcell's finding.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'the Aztecs would have disputed the idea that the morality of an individual's actions can be assessed by appealing to standards of behavior that are independent of the individual's social circumstances': directly follows from Purcell — if Aztec ethics ties morality to social role and community, Aztecs would reject context-independent moral standards. ✓
  • Choice B — 'the Aztecs would not have accepted the notion that the morality of an individual's actions can be fairly evaluated by people who do not live in the same society': the passage is about whether context matters, not about who is qualified to judge. This adds a claim not in the text. ✗
  • Choice C — 'actions by members of Aztec society who contributed a great deal to their community could be judged as morally good even if those actions were inconsistent with behaviors the Aztecs regarded as good in all contexts': contradicts the passage — the Aztecs didn't regard any behaviors as good "in all contexts." ✗
  • Choice D — 'similar actions performed by people in different social roles in Aztec society would have been regarded as morally equivalent unless those actions led to different outcomes for the community': the passage says role determines moral judgment, so similar actions by people in different roles would not be equivalent by default — this misreads the framework. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
